conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I have a child - my son Alex. He's 17 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! 

To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask a few more questions.

Is Alex currently in full-time non-advanced education or approved training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in education at the moment. He finished school but he's not doing any courses or training right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

Can you tell me - is Alex currently working, and if so, how many hours per week does he work?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not working at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information.

Just to confirm - is Alex registered with the armed forces or with a government-sponsored careers service, or is he currently claiming any benefits in his own right (such as Universal Credit or Jobseeker's Allowance)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not registered with the careers service or the armed forces, and he's not claiming any benefits in his own name either.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information.

Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

1. Are you the person responsible for Alex - meaning you live with him and are responsible for his care?
2. Are you currently living in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives at home with me and I'm responsible for him. And yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

One final question: Do you know when Alex's 17th birthday was? Specifically, has he already reached the first Monday in September following his 16th birthday (often called the "September following 16th birthday" date)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's 17 now, so he's definitely past that date - he turned 16 more than a year ago.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why: Since Alex is 17 years old (and has passed the first Monday in September following his 16th birthday), he would only qualify for Child Benefit if he were in approved full-time non-advanced education or training. As Alex is not currently in education, training, registered with a careers service, or in the armed forces, he does not meet the qualifying conditions for Child Benefit for young people aged 16-19.

**However**, if Alex's situation changes and he enrolls in full-time education or approved training in the future, you would be able to claim Child Benefit for him at that point (up until he turns 20 or leaves education/training, whichever comes first).

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and has passed the first Monday in September following his 16th birthday. Under Child Benefit rules, you can only claim for a child aged 16 or over if they are in approved full-time non-advanced education or training. Since Alex is not currently in education, training, registered with the careers service, or in the armed forces, he does not meet the qualifying conditions for Child Benefit for young people aged 16-19.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is responsible for their 17-year-old son Alex who lives with them in the UK. However, Alex is not in full-time education or approved training, which is a requirement for Child Benefit eligibility for young people aged 16 and over. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
